skill.name: mtls-configuration
skill.description: Guide service teams through mutual TLS setup, certificate management, and troubleshooting for Istio, Linkerd, SPIFFE, SPIRE, and cert-manager environments.
skill.summary: Configure and troubleshoot mTLS for zero-trust service communication.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged many Markdown code fences as Ruby backtick execution, which is a false positive for this documentation-only skill. Real risk remains because several copy-pastable examples permit, disable, or bypass mTLS and include certificate file paths that require careful secret handling.

medium_findings.title: Copy-pastable mTLS exceptions can weaken production service protection
medium_findings.description: The skill includes examples for PERMISSIVE mode, a disabled metrics port, and skipped Linkerd outbound ports. These are legitimate migration and exception patterns, but they can reduce service-to-service authentication if copied without strict scope and review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly configure weaker or bypass behavior. The surrounding text presents them as templates, so misuse is plausible even though there is no malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: External TLS example does not use mutual authentication
medium_findings.description: The external service example uses SIMPLE TLS with only a CA certificate. This may be appropriate for some outbound calls, but it does not provide mTLS client authentication and may conflict with the skill's mTLS focus.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The configuration clearly uses mode SIMPLE, followed by a separate MUTUAL example. This is a confirmed weaker pattern, but it can be legitimate for non-mTLS external services.

low_findings.title: Markdown code fences misclassified as Ruby backtick execution
low_findings.description: The static external command findings at the template and diagram sections are false positives. They are Markdown fences that contain diagrams, YAML, or shell examples, not executable Ruby code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review shows fenced Markdown blocks, not Ruby execution. No file contains runnable Ruby code or a command execution wrapper.

low_findings.title: Certificate and private key paths are placeholders, not embedded secrets
low_findings.description: The skill references certificate paths and placeholder encoded certificate material. No real private key or certificate value is present, but users must avoid committing real key material when adapting the examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced values are file paths or angle-bracket placeholders. This confirms the static sensitive-data findings are not leaked credentials.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs and IP address are documentation references or bind examples
low_findings.description: The URLs point to public documentation resources, and the 0.0.0.0 value appears in a SPIRE server bind address example. There is no evidence of data exfiltration or unauthorized network access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded network indicators are visible documentation or configuration examples. No code sends data to these URLs or addresses.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography findings are terminology and Kubernetes resource matches
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography hits correspond to mTLS description text and Kubernetes DestinationRule examples. I found no MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic primitive in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found security configuration terms rather than weak algorithms. The finding is likely caused by broad keyword matching.

dangerous_patterns.title: mTLS bypass or downgrade configuration examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples include PERMISSIVE mode, DISABLE on a port, and skip-outbound-ports. These patterns should be documented as migration-only or exception-only settings.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The risky configuration modes are explicit in the templates. The risk depends on how users apply them.

dangerous_patterns.title: Private key handling in reusable templates
dangerous_patterns.description: Reusable templates reference private key files and TLS secrets. This is expected for mTLS, but it requires secret-manager integration and strict access controls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill necessarily handles certificate paths and key references. The content uses placeholders, so the pattern is risky but not evidence of leakage.

content.user_title: Configure Mutual TLS for Service Meshes
content.value_statement: Service teams need consistent mTLS patterns across service meshes and Kubernetes clusters. This skill provides practical templates, rotation guidance, and troubleshooting steps for secure service communication.
actual_capabilities: Explains mutual TLS flow and certificate hierarchy for service-to-service traffic.
Provides Istio PeerAuthentication and DestinationRule examples for strict and mutual TLS.
Shows cert-manager certificate issuer and workload certificate patterns.
Documents SPIFFE and SPIRE integration concepts for workload identities.
Includes command examples for certificate expiry checks and mTLS troubleshooting.
Lists operational best practices for certificate rotation and TLS error monitoring.
limitations: It is a documentation skill and does not deploy or verify resources automatically.
Examples require review before use in production namespaces or shared clusters.
It does not generate real certificates, keys, or certificate authority material.
It does not replace vendor documentation for version-specific service mesh behavior.

use_cases.title: Plan a service mesh mTLS rollout
use_cases.description: Use the templates and migration guidance to move namespaces from permissive mode to strict mTLS.

use_cases.title: Standardize workload certificate management
use_cases.description: Adapt the cert-manager and SPIRE examples to define issuer, secret, and workload identity patterns.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot failed service handshakes
use_cases.description: Use the listed Istio and Linkerd checks to inspect policies, destination rules, proxies, and certificates.

prompt_templates.title: Explain mTLS basics
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how mutual TLS works for service-to-service communication in Kubernetes. Include certificate roles and common failure points.

prompt_templates.title: Create an Istio rollout plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a staged Istio mTLS rollout plan for my namespace. Include checks before moving from permissive mode to strict mode.

prompt_templates.title: Review certificate management
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my planned cert-manager and SPIRE approach for workload certificates. Identify rotation, secret storage, and trust-domain risks.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose a mesh outage
prompt_templates.prompt: Help diagnose an mTLS outage between two services. Ask for the policy, destination rule, proxy logs, and certificate details you need.

output_examples.input: We need to migrate a legacy namespace to strict mTLS.
output_examples.output: A staged plan that starts with permissive mode, inventories non-mesh clients, validates telemetry, scopes exceptions, and defines rollback checks.

output_examples.input: A service cannot connect after enabling Istio mTLS.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting checklist covering PeerAuthentication, DestinationRule settings, proxy certificate state, sidecar injection, and TLS error logs.
A certificate management outline with issuer selection, renewal windows, secret handling rules, expiry alerts, and rotation validation steps.

best_practices: Treat permissive mode and disabled ports as temporary exceptions with owners and expiry dates.
Store private keys in approved secret managers and restrict Kubernetes secret access.
Validate certificate expiry, trust domains, and policy state before enforcing strict mTLS.
anti_patterns: Do not copy bypass examples into production without documented risk acceptance.
Do not commit real certificates, private keys, or encoded secret data to source control.
Do not assume all external TLS traffic provides mutual authentication.

faq.question: Does this skill configure mTLS automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and templates that an operator must adapt, apply, and verify.

faq.question: Which service meshes does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers Istio and Linkerd examples, plus SPIFFE, SPIRE, and cert-manager concepts.

faq.question: Can I use the examples in production?
faq.answer: Yes, after review. Replace placeholders, scope exceptions, and validate behavior in a test environment first.

faq.question: Does it include real private keys?
faq.answer: No. It uses placeholder paths and placeholder encoded values for certificate examples.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but some examples weaken mTLS if copied without controls.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
